diagrams.title: Memory Hierarchy Pyramid
diagrams.description: A vertical pyramid diagram showing the hierarchical structure of computer memory from fastest/smallest at top to slowest/largest at bottom. The pyramid is divided into levels: Registers at apex, then L1/L2/L3 Cache, followed by Main Memory (RAM), then SSD/Flash Storage, and finally HDD/Magnetic Storage at the base. Arrows or annotations show the direction of data flow and the trade-off between speed and capacity at each level.
elements: L1/L2/L3 Cache (KB to MB, nanosecond access)
Solid State Drive (GB to TB, microsecond to millisecond)
diagrams.purpose: Visualizes the fundamental trade-off in computer architecture between speed and capacity, helping students understand why caches are necessary and how they fit into the overall memory hierarchy.

diagrams.title: Cache Memory Structure and Addressing
diagrams.description: A diagram showing the internal organization of a cache memory block. It displays a main memory address divided into three fields: Tag, Index, and Offset. Below this, a cache memory structure shows cache lines/blocks with fields for Valid bit, Tag, and Data. Lines connect the address fields to their corresponding cache locations to illustrate how a CPU address is used to access cached data.
elements: Cache line structure (Valid bit, Tag, Data block)
Index field mapping to cache set
diagrams.purpose: Explains how cache memory is physically organized and how memory addresses are interpreted to locate data in the cache, which is fundamental to understanding cache hits, misses, and the importance of locality.

diagrams.title: Cache Mapping Techniques Comparison
diagrams.description: A three-panel diagram comparing the three main cache mapping techniques side by side. Panel 1 shows Direct Mapped Cache where each memory block maps to exactly one cache line. Panel 2 shows Fully Associative Cache where any memory block can go anywhere in cache. Panel 3 shows Set-Associative Cache (e.g., 4-way) where each block maps to a specific set but can occupy any line within that set. Each panel shows memory blocks, cache lines, and demonstrates placement/collision scenarios.
elements: Direct Mapped panel with memory-to-cache mapping
Fully Associative panel with flexibility shown
Set-Associative panel showing sets and ways
diagrams.purpose: Provides a visual comparison of cache mapping strategies, helping students understand the trade-offs between complexity, flexibility, and collision rates that affect cache performance.
